topics: What seals are and why they matter
How to design and make your own seal
Printing with seals and block printing traditions
subject: Arts

sections.heading: 🔍 The Quest Begins — What Are Seals?
sections.body: A seal is like a special stamp that carries a picture, symbol or text. It is used to show who someone is or to make something official — just like when your school principal stamps a document to make it real and important. Seals have been used for thousands of years by kings, kingdoms and organisations to share information about who they are and what they believe in. Think of it like a superhero's logo — one look and you know who it belongs to!
keyPoints: Seals can have pictures, symbols or written text on them
They tell us about a person's identity, power or organisation
Seals have been used across many periods of history

sections.heading: 🧙 Wizard's Eye — Observing Seals Around You
sections.body: Before you create your own seal, you need to look carefully at seals that already exist in the world around you. Your school principal's rubber stamp, the seal on your report card, or even the mark stamped on a postal envelope — all of these are seals! When you visit a post office, you can see the postal department's special logo and seal up close. Even coins are a kind of seal — try placing paper over a coin and rubbing a pencil over it to magically reveal the design hidden on it!
keyPoints: Seals are all around us — on documents, envelopes and coins
The postal department uses seals with its own symbol and information
Rubbing a pencil over a coin on paper helps you see the seal design clearly

sections.heading: ⚔️ Crafting Your Power — Designing Your Own Seal
sections.body: Now comes the exciting part of the quest — making your very own seal! First, think of a simple symbol that represents YOU. It could be your favourite animal, a cricket bat, a mango, a flower — anything that shows your personality or values. Keep the design simple because simple designs make the clearest impressions. Then choose your material — clay, cardboard, sponge or even jute rope — and carefully shape your design into a flat, even surface so it stamps neatly.
keyPoints: Your personal seal should have a simple symbol that represents you
Materials like clay, cardboard and sponge work great for making seals
A flat and even design gives the clearest print

sections.heading: 🐉 Taming the Dragon — Testing and Printing Your Seal
sections.body: Once your seal is ready, it is time to test its power! Press a flat seal into paint or ink and stamp it on paper to see the impression it makes. If the print is not clear, you can fix the design and try again — even the greatest wizards practise before they master a spell! A seal can be used again and again, just like a rubber stamp, which is what makes it so magical and useful.
keyPoints: Apply paint or ink to your seal and press it on paper to test it
You can improve your seal design based on what the test print looks like
A seal can be used repeatedly to make the same design many times

sections.heading: 🌟 Realm of Mastery — Ajrakh Block Printing
sections.body: Your seal is actually very similar to a wooden block used by traditional artists in India! In Kutchh, Gujarat, skilled craftspeople use beautifully carved wooden blocks to print stunning patterns on cloth — this tradition is called Ajrakh block printing. The raised part of the block picks up colour and stamps it on the fabric, just like your seal picks up ink and stamps on paper. By stamping the same block again and again in a pattern, they create gorgeous textiles that are famous all over the world. You can try the same thing with your seal to make your own pattern!
keyPoints: Ajrakh is a traditional hand block printing art from Kutchh, Gujarat
A carved wooden block is inked and pressed on cloth to create patterns
Repeating a seal design creates a beautiful continuous pattern — just like a real block print!

summary: In this chapter, young heroes set out on a quest to discover the magical world of seals and printing! They learned that seals are stamps with pictures or symbols that tell us about a person, kingdom or organisation, and have been used throughout history. They explored seals around them — at school, the post office and even on coins — before designing and crafting their very own personal seal using simple materials like clay, cardboard or sponge. After testing and improving their seals by printing them on paper, they connected this process to the ancient and beautiful tradition of Ajrakh block printing from Gujarat, where skilled artists use carved wooden blocks to create stunning patterns on fabric. Every young artist now has the power and mastery to create their own unique prints!
